_metadata.description: Regulatory allergen identification database for supplement ingredients. Covers FDA major allergens (9) and EU Annex II allergens (8). Used for consumer warnings and on-device personalization — not for general score penalties.

scoring_system.rule: All entries are flag_only — no score deductions. severity_level is used for warning priority and on-device personalization only. Severity tiers: high = documented fatal anaphylaxis risk; moderate = significant IgE reactions / celiac trigger; low = usually mild reactions.

_metadata.regulatory_note: Strictly limited to FDA FALCPA/FASTER Act major allergens and EU Annex II allergens. Non-regulatory sensitivities (nightshades, latex cross-reactivity, corn, coconut, etc.) are NOT included — those belong in consumer preference profiles, not allergen flagging. Transparency penalties (natural/artificial flavors) belong in harmful_additives or other_ingredients.

fields.severity_level: Warning priority for on-device personalization. Values: low, moderate, high.
fields.aliases: Case-insensitive matching. MUST NOT include IQM-scorable ingredient names — allergen check runs before IQM lookup in the normalizer and would hijack ingredient identity.
fields.prevalence: How commonly this allergen appears in supplements: low, moderate, high.
fields.CUI: UMLS Concept Unique Identifier. null for composite groups spanning multiple species.

allergens.supplement_context: Common emulsifier/excipient and in protein products.
allergens.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Soy lecithin is the most common form in supplements, used as emulsifier in softgels and powders. Highly refined soy oil may not trigger reactions but must still be labeled. Cross-reactivity with other legumes (peanut, lupin) occurs in 5-10% of soy-allergic individuals. Fermented soy (natto) retains allergenic proteins.

allergens.supplement_context: Common in protein supplements, some probiotics, and tablets/capsules (lactose).
allergens.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Severity moderate: milk is the 3rd most common cause of pediatric food anaphylaxis (after peanut and tree nuts). Casein and whey are the two primary allergenic protein fractions; casein allergy tends to be more persistent and severe. Lactose intolerance (non-immune) is distinct from milk allergy (IgE-mediated) but both require flagging. Probiotic strains grown on milk media may carry trace proteins.

allergens.supplement_context: Sometimes in protein powders and specialty formulas.
allergens.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Severity moderate: egg allergy can cause anaphylaxis, especially in children; ovomucoid (Gal d 1) is heat-stable and survives cooking/processing. Ovalbumin (egg white) is the dominant allergen by mass. Lysozyme (muramidase) is egg white-derived but excluded from aliases (IQM-scored). Bare 'albumin' excluded — bovine serum albumin and plant albumins are not egg allergens.

FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Severity high: tree nuts are the 2nd leading cause of fatal food-induced anaphylaxis after peanuts. Cashew and walnut are the most commonly implicated species. Cross-reactivity between tree nut species is common (cashew-pistachio, walnut-pecan). Nut oils retain allergenic proteins unless highly refined. Shea nut (Vitellaria paradoxa) is FDA-classified as tree nut.

allergens.supplement_context: Oils/extracts; high-risk allergen for sensitive individuals.
allergens.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Peanut allergy is the leading cause of food-induced anaphylaxis in the US. Ara h 1, h 2, h 3 are the major allergenic proteins; they survive roasting and processing. Cross-reactivity with lupin (30-50%) and other legumes (soy, lentil) is clinically significant. Even highly refined peanut oil may contain trace proteins in supplement contexts.

allergens.supplement_context: Very common in omega-3 supplements and some vitamin A/D sources.
allergens.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Parvalbumin is the major fish allergen; it is heat-stable and present across most bony fish species. Fish collagen/gelatin retains allergenic potential. IQM-scored fish derivatives (fish oil, cod liver oil, salmon oil, marine collagen) are excluded from aliases to preserve IQM identity — they are flagged via the enricher's allergen cross-reference, not normalizer interception.

allergens.supplement_context: Glucosamine often derived from shellfish; krill oil is crustacean-derived.
allergens.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Tropomyosin is the major crustacean allergen; it cross-reacts with dust mite and cockroach tropomyosin. Chitosan (deacetylated chitin from shells) retains allergenic risk. Krill oil is excluded from aliases (IQM-scored) but is crustacean-derived. Glucosamine from shellfish shells may contain trace tropomyosin. Shellfish allergy affects ~2% of adults and can cause severe anaphylaxis.

allergens.supplement_context: May indicate gluten presence; important for celiac/gluten sensitivity.
allergens.notes: FDA major allergen (FALCPA). Wheat allergy (IgE-mediated) is distinct from celiac disease (autoimmune) but both require flagging. Gluten proteins (gliadin/glutenin) are the primary triggers. Spelt, kamut, einkorn, emmer, and farro are wheat species that contain gluten. Triticale is a wheat-rye hybrid. Wheat germ oil is typically low in protein but not guaranteed gluten-free.

allergens.supplement_context: Can appear as oil/extract; major allergen.
allergens.notes: FDA major allergen as of January 2023 (FASTER Act). Ses i 1 is the major allergenic protein. Sesame allergy prevalence is rising, estimated at 0.2% in the US. Sesamin and sesamolin (lignan compounds) are excluded from aliases because they have IQM entries. Cross-reactivity with poppy seed, kiwi, and tree nuts has been reported.

allergens.supplement_context: May appear in greens powders, malt-based excipients, or flavor bases.
allergens.notes: EU Annex II allergen (cereals containing gluten). Barley contains hordein, a gluten-type prolamin toxic to celiac patients. Malt and malt extract are barley-derived and contain gluten. Barley grass harvested pre-jointing may be low in gluten but is still flagged. Barley beta-glucan (a soluble fiber) is used in heart-health supplements and retains the grain origin.

allergens.supplement_context: Less common; may appear in fiber blends or flavor carriers.
allergens.notes: EU Annex II allergen (cereals containing gluten). Rye contains secalin, a gluten-type prolamin that triggers celiac disease. Rye fiber supplements may carry residual gluten. Cross-reactivity with wheat is very high due to prolamin homology. Triticale (wheat-rye hybrid) is listed under wheat but also relevant for rye-allergic individuals.

allergens.supplement_context: Common in meal replacements/protein blends; gluten cross-contact possible.
allergens.notes: EU Annex II allergen (cereals containing gluten). Avenin is the oat prolamin; most celiac patients tolerate pure oats (Codex threshold <20 ppm), but 5-10% react to avenin itself. Commercial oats are frequently cross-contaminated with wheat/barley. Oat beta-glucan and oat straw extract are excluded from aliases (IQM-scored) but retain grain origin for allergen context.

allergens.supplement_context: Less common; found in some marine-based supplements.
allergens.notes: EU Annex II major allergen. Tropomyosin is the primary mollusc allergen, homologous to crustacean tropomyosin — cross-reactivity between molluscs and crustaceans is 10-30%. Green-lipped mussel (Perna canaliculus) extract is a popular joint supplement (IQM-scored, excluded from aliases). Oyster extract and pearl powder are used in mineral/beauty supplements.

allergens.supplement_context: Can trigger reactions in sensitive individuals (notably asthma).
allergens.notes: EU Annex II allergen and FDA-regulated preservative (>10 ppm requires labeling). Sulfite sensitivity affects 5-10% of asthmatics, manifesting as bronchospasm, urticaria, or anaphylaxis. Mechanism involves SO2 inhalation from acidic gastric conversion. E220-E228 are EU additive codes for sulfiting agents. UK/EU spelling variants (sulphites, sulphur dioxide) included for international label matching.

EU Annex II allergen. Api g 1 is the major celery allergen, homologous to birch pollen Bet v 1 — celery allergy is strongly associated with birch pollen sensitization (celery-mugwort-spice syndrome). Celery seed extract is used in joint/blood-pressure supplements (IQM-scored, excluded from aliases). Reactions can range from OAS (oral allergy syndrome) to severe anaphylaxis.

EU Annex II allergen. Sin a 1 (yellow mustard) and Bra j 1 (brown/black mustard) are the major allergens; both are heat-stable and survive processing. Mustard seed oil is used in some Ayurvedic and topical supplement formulations. Cross-reactivity within Brassicaceae family (rapeseed, cabbage) is possible. Mustard allergy can cause severe anaphylaxis, particularly in France and Spain.

allergens.supplement_context: Rare; EU allergen; cross-reactivity possible with peanut allergy.
allergens.notes: EU Annex II allergen. Lupin (Lupinus spp.) is a legume with strong cross-reactivity to peanut — 30-50% of peanut-allergic individuals react to lupin. Lupin protein is emerging as a plant protein source in vegan supplements. The allergenic proteins (conglutins) survive thermal processing. Lupin flour may appear as an excipient. Mandatory labeling in EU; not yet an FDA major allergen.
